coredata.dc:title: Novel ratiometric probe based on the use of rare earth-carbon dots nanocomposite for the visual determination of doxycycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xiaolin
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Rare earth-carbon dots (RE-CDs) hybrid nanomaterials with the merits of both RE and CDs have rapidly emerging as highly promising functional materials in biochemical analysis. In this work, a new kind of water-soluble RE-CDs nanocomposite (CDs@CaF2:Eu3+) was developed for the ratiometric determination of doxycycline (DOX). The CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ under the excitation at 365 nm displayed blue emission of CDs at 440 nm and no obvious emission of Eu3+. With the addition of DOX, substantial fluorescence quenching of the CDs at 440 nm and enhancement of Eu3+ at 613 nm were observed, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent response toward DOX. A wide linear range from 0.1 µM to 30 µM was achieved in the detection of DOX with a lowest detection limit of 43 nM. In particular, the probe could discriminate DOX from other tetracycline antibiotics through unique fluorescence response. Moreover, we have successfully applied the method for the determination of DOX in milk and honey samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food analysis
Rare earth
Carbon dots
Doxycycline
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of DOX in actual samples
Determination of DOX in milk and honey samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (Inset: HRTEM image of CDs, size distribution histogram of CDs). (b) TEM image of CaF2:Eu3+. (c) TEM image of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ (d) SEM image of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+.
(a) XRD patterns of CDs, CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3+. (b) The main peak amplification XRD patterns of CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3+.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, CaF2:Eu3+, CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3++DOX. (b) UV–vis spectra of CDs, CDs, CaF2:Eu3+, DOX, CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3++DOX.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ in the presence of DOX with different concentrations (Inset: photograph of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ under irradiation with UV light at 365 nm when DOX concentration is varied [(0, 0.1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 20, 30 μM) from left to the right]). (b) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F613/F440) of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+and the concentrations of DOX.

(a) Selectivity and anti-interference of DOX detection. (b) Fluorescence spectra of DOX (black line), TC (red line), OTC (blue line), CTC (green line) and MOC (purple line) with (solid line) and without (dotted line) CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ under optimal conditions (Inset: photograph of TCs with and without CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ under irradiation with UV light at 365 nm).
Schematic illustration of DOX detection based on CDs@CaF2:Eu3+.
Detection of DOX in milk and honey samples using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Novel ratiometric probe based on the use of rare earth-carbon dots nanocomposite for the visual determination of doxycycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of DOX detection based on CDs@CaF2:Eu3+.

Rare earth-carbon dots (RE-CDs) hybrid nanomaterials with the merits of both RE and CDs have rapidly emerging as highly promising functional materials in biochemical analysis. In this work, a new kind of water-soluble RE-CDs nanocomposite (CDs@CaF2:Eu3+) was developed for the ratiometric determination of doxycycline (DOX). The CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ under the excitation at 365 nm displayed blue emission of CDs at 440 nm and no obvious emission of Eu3+. With the addition of DOX, substantial fluorescence quenching of the CDs at 440 nm and enhancement of Eu3+ at 613 nm were observed, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent response toward DOX. A wide linear range from 0.1 µM to 30 µM was achieved in the detection of DOX with a lowest detection limit of 43 nM. In particular, the probe could discriminate DOX from other tetracycline antibiotics through unique fluorescence response. Moreover, we have successfully applied the method for the determination of DOX in milk and honey samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, rare earth - carbon dots (RE-CDs) hybrid nanomaterials have attracted tremendous attentions owing to their captivating properties and wide applications in biochemical analysis fields [1]. Differ from the common misconception, rare earth (RE) elements are with abundant storage in the earth's crust, which are known as “ jewels for functional materials of the future” [2,3]. The gradual occupation of 4f orbitals makes them potential luminescent materials with large Stokes and/or anti-Stokes shifts, long luminescence lifetimes, and narrow emission bands [4,5]. However, it’s worth noting that the overall properties and water solubility of single-component RE materials are insufficient to meet the requirements of multi-functional applications. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging group of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, which we usually define as discrete zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with sizes less than 10 nm [6–8]. The easily embellished functional groups on CDs make them excellent nanocarriers with outstanding water-solubility, biocompatibility and tunability [1,9]. Thus, CDs have been used in biomedicine [10], chemiluminescence [11,12], sensors [13,14], etc. RE-CDs hybrid nanomaterials combined RE and CDs which greatly enhance their overall performance, especially luminescence, and thus bring very promising practical applications in sensing fields [1].

With the enhancement of people's awareness of food safety, the domestic and international requirements for the maximum limit of veterinary drug residues in foodstuffs are increasingly stringent [15]. As one of tetracycline veterinary drugs, Doxycycline (DOX) has a wide range of antimicrobial activities against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [16]. Due to its broad-spectrum activity and cost-effectiveness, DOX is widely used in the treatment of animal husbandry and as feed additives to promote the growth of animals. On the other hand, the abuse of DOX will lead to widespread antibiotic residues in food, such as honey, meat and milk, which may cause serious harm to human health [17]. There is now an urgent need for a rapid, sensitive, specific, and inexpensive method for DOX detection. Up to now, numerous studies have been examined to find effective methods for DOX detection, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with UV detection [16,18], high performance thin layer chromatography [19], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [20], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [21], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [22]. Optical methods, especially fluorometry, are superior to the others owing to their high sensitivity, good selectivity and fast response [23,24]. A series of fluorescent sensors, such as CDs [25,26] and metal–organic coordination polymers [27], have been designed to detect DOX. In addition, several fluorescence sensors based on RE-CDs hybrid nanomaterials have also been reported recently, realizing the visual ratiometric detection of tetracyclines (TCs) [28–30]. Although a number of methods to perform assays of DOX using fluorescent probes have been reported, structural similarity is still the main challenge in discriminating and detecting DOX. Besides, the water-solubility of fluorescence probes also needs to be improved urgently.

Herein, we prepared CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ nanocomposite with excellent water-solubility for selective and quantitative detection of DOX. First, we noted that calcium fluoride (CaF2) is an ideal host material for luminescent RE ions due to its low phonon energy (~328 cm-1) and subsequent minimizes the excited state quenching of RE ions. Besides, CaF2 nanoparticles also have good thermal stability and environmental stability [31]. Based on this, CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ nanocomposite were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method. Moreover, the CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ probe has a strong CDs characteristic emission peak at 440 nm, and no obvious fluorescence of Eu3+ can be observed. With the addition of DOX, the fluorescence of Eu3+ increased, while the fluorescence of CDs quenched. Thus, the visible color of fluorescence changes from blue to red (Scheme 1 ). Most importantly, the probe can specifically recognize DOX and other TCs by displaying different fluorescence response signals.Therefore, the CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescence sensor is applicable for the quantitative detection of DOX in foods.

Ammonium fluoride (NH4F), calcium chloride (CaCl2), europium chloride (EuCl3·6H2O), doxycycline (DOX) and tetracycline (TC) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), hydroxyethyl piperazine-N’-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), aluminum chloride (AlCl3), leucine (Leu), proline (Pro), lysine (Lys), vitamin C (VC), and vitamin B1 (VB1) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Urea (CH4N2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium chloride (KCl), trichloroacetic acid (C2HCl3O2), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, C10H14N2Na2O8) were purchased from Guoyao Industry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis Bag (MW:1000) was provided by Union CAR-Bide Co. (USA). The pH series of HEPES buffer were prepared by 50 mM HEPES solution, 4 M NaOH solution and 5% HCl solution. The method to prepare McLlvaine buffer (pH 4) was as follow: Na2HPO4·12H2O (27.6 g), C6H8O7·H2O (12.9 g) and EDTA-2Na (37.2 g), dissolved in water and diluted at a constant volume of 1000 mL. The reagents that used in the experiment were all analytically pure. The ultrapure water used in the experiment was obtained by the United States Milli-Q purification system.

The morphology was observed with a Tecnai G 2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Equipment Co., Ltd., USA) and a JSM-7500F scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7500F, Japan Electronics Corporation). X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were determined by X-ray powder diffractometer of Neo-Science Corporation, Japan. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was obtained by scanning on the Niocolet 380 spectrometer of Var-ian Equipment Inc., USA. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by the Thermo Escalab 250Xi instrument of the American Thermoelectric Company. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained by the Cary 300 UV/Vis spectrophotometer, Var-ian Equipment, Inc., USA. Fluorescence spectra were obtained by scanning with the LS-55 fluorescence/phosphorescence spectrophotometer from Perkin-Elmer, USA. Fluorescence lifetime was determined by JY-Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer.

NH4F (0.29 g) and CDs (0.025 g) were dissolved in 30 mL methanol, and heated to 70 ℃. Then 3 mL methanol solution (containing 0.51 g CaCl2 and 0.073 g EuCl3·6H2O) was added dropwise to the above solution, stirring at 70 ℃ for 2 h in a reflux system. The resulting solution cooled to room temperature, and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min to collect precipitates. And precipitates were then washed methanol for several times and dried under vacuum at 50℃. The synthesis of CaF2:Eu3+ nanoparticles was similar to the above steps, which was referenced from the literature [32]. The synthesis of CDs can be found in the Supplementary data.

Firstly, a reaction mixture containing 100 μL CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ (10 mg/mL), 1 mL HEPES buffer (pH 7) and 3 mL ultrapure water was first prepared. Subsequently, different concentrations of DOX were added into the above solution and then were diluted to 5 mL. After incubating at room temperature for 15 min, and fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 365 nm and emission wavelength of 440 nm and 613 nm. The F613/F440 was used to calculate the signal output value for the experimental data. All experiments data were obtained by taking the average of three replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk and honey were chosen to evaluate the feasibility and accuracy of fluorescent sensing with the CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ sensor for real applications. The practicability of the probe was verified by the standard addition method. The food samples were treated according to the previous reports [28,33]. 10 mL liquid milk was taken and mixed with 2 mL trichloroacetic acid (0.61 M) and sonicated for 20 min. After centrifugation for 10 min at 7000 rpm to remove proteins, the supernatant was filtered by using a 0.22 μm organic membrane. Finally, the sample solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 with NaOH solution and kept in the fridge for future use. 30 mL of McLlvaine Buffer (pH 4) was introduced into 10 g honey and sonicated for 20 min. After centrifugation for 10 min at 7000 rpm to remove proteins, the supernatant was filtered by using a 0.22 μm organic membrane. Finally, the sample solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 with NaOH solution and kept in the fridge for future use. All food samples were bought from supermarkets in Linfen City, China.

The morphology and particle size distribution of CDs, CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were characterized by TEM and SEM. The TEM image shows that the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1 a) were quasi-spherical dots and well dispersed. The particle size statistics (inset of Fig. 1a) reveal that the diameters of CDs were mainly distributed in the 1.39–8.24 nm range with a mean size of 4.12 nm. The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) shows that the CDs had obvious lattice fringes, indicating that the as-prepared CDs posed a graphite-like structure. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of CaF2:Eu3+. The nanoparticles were nearly spherical but bad dispersed, possibly due to the reaction solvent and temperature during synthesis. The SEM image shows the CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ was nanoparticles composite, and adhesion between nanoparticles was commonly existed, which can also be seen in the TEM image (Fig. 1c). This phenomenon illustrates that the binding method of CaF2:Eu3+ nanoparticles and CDs may be encapsulation or adhesion.

The crystalline phase of CDs, CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ was verified by the XRD spectrum (Fig. 2 a). The XRD pattern of the CDs shows a diffuse peak centered at 2θ = 24.2°, which again confirms the graphite-like structure of CDs. The diffraction peaks of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were consistent with that of CaF2:Eu3+, indicating that the addition of CDs did not change the crystal structure of CaF2. Besides, the Fig. 2b shows that the diffraction peak corresponding to (111) plane of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ was slightly biased to a small angular direction, owning to the lattice expansion of the cubic crystal with doping CDs. This phenomenon confirms the formation of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ nanocomposites [34].

The FTIR spectra of CDs, CaF2:Eu3+, CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were displayed in Fig. 3 a. For free CDs, four characteristic peaks at 3198, 1675, 1571 and 1403 cm−1 were assigned to the OH, C = O, C-N, and N-H stretching vibration, suggesting that carboxylic group and amino group were distributed on the surface of CDs. After being incorporated into CaF2:Eu3+, the characteristic peaks of the CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were comparatively similar to that of CaF2:Eu3+, suggesting the amount of CDs encapsulated or attached to the CaF2:Eu3+ nanoparticles was relatively limited. Moreover, the characteristic OH band shifted from 3424 to 3438 cm−1, suggesting CDs assembling into CaF2:Eu3+ due to the hydrogen bond interaction.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CDs, CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were shown in Fig. 3b. The weak shoulder peak of CDs at 242 nm was attributed to n → π* transition of the oxygen-containing groups, while the strong absorption peak at 350 nm may be caused by the n-π* transitions [35]. CaF2:Eu3+ has no obvious absorption peak in the UV–visible region. The strong absorption peak of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ at 334 nm presents a blue shift of 16 nm in contrast to that of CDs. However, under 365 nm excitation, the optimal emission wavelength does not change, which may be due to the selective absorption of excitation light in the protective layer of CaF2 when ultraviolet radiation reaches CDs [34]. This further proves that there is a small amount of CDs encapsulated or attached to CaF2 particles.

The surface composition and elements of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 4 , Fig. S1). CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ is composed of C, N, O, Ca, F and Eu respectively. High-resolution Ca and F spectra confirmed the synthesis of CaF2 [36], high-resolution Eu spectra confirmed that the valence state of Eu is Eu3+, and C, O and N spectra further confirmed that CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ contains abundant surface groups, such as –COOH, –OH and –NH2, indicating good water solubility, have the possibility of application in sensing.

Upon the addition of DOX, the fluorescence of Eu3+ in CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ was enhanced, while the fluorescence of CDs was weakened. To explore the reaction mechanism, firstly, we compared the UV–Vis absorption spectra of DOX, and the excitation and emission spectra of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+. The results in Fig. S2a reveal quite a precise spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of DOX and the excitation spectrum and emission spectra of CDs in CDs@CaF2:Eu3+, denoting that the quenching behavior of DOX toward the fluorescence of CDs can be attributed to inner filter effect (IFE). Furthermore, the CDs lifetime was determined in CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ solution by without and with DOX. The results suggested (Fig. S2b) that after DOX addition, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs in CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ has no obvious change (from 6.75 ns to 6.83 ns), also confirming the quenching mechanism of IFE [37]. Secondly, we compared the UV–Vis spectrum of CDs, CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3++DOX. As shown in Fig. 3b, CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ has a maximum absorption peak at 334 nm, while in the presence of DOX, a new absorption peak at 383 nm appeared, indicating the formation of new complex between DOX and the probe. Considering that DOX is a common ligand of Eu3+and fluorescence enhancement of Eu3+ at 613 nm can be observed with the addition of DOX, the new peak can basically be attributed to DOX-Eu3+ [38]. To further identify the interaction between the Eu3+ and DOX, the FTIR spectra of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2:Eu3++DOX have been carried out. As shown in Fig. 3a, compared with the FTIR spectrum of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+, that of CDs@CaF2:Eu3++DOX has a blue shift or red shift of the adsorption peak at –OH (from 3438 cm to 1 to 3385 cm-1), –COOH(from 1636 cm to 1 to 1608 cm-1), and C = O (from 1407 cm to 1 to 1400 cm-1). These results indicated that O in carbonyl group of DOX could specifically chelate with Eu3+ to produce antenna effect by energy transfer from DOX to Eu3+, which caused the fluorescence enhancement of Eu3+ [27,39].

To achieve the best sensing performance of the probe, we optimized main factors that might impact the quenching efficiency, including reaction time and pH value. As shown in Fig. S3, we discovered that the reaction efficiency of the system tended to be stable after the addition of DOX for 15 min. The value of F613/F440 did not increase anymore but reached an equilibrium state, suggesting that the reaction of the system tended to be saturated. Therefore, 15 min was selected as the incubation time for subsequent experiments. Next, the influence of pH (5.0–9.0) on fluorescence intensity of the probe was studied and shown in Fig. S4a. It can be observed that in alkaline environment, DOX itself will produce fluorescence, leading to interference to the system. Additionally, considering that our output signal is F613/F440, the reaction efficiency of the system is obviously the highest at pH 7. Besides, although the fluorescence intensity of the interference decreased after incubating at room temperature for 15 min, the F613/F440 is still the highest at pH 7 (Fig. S4b). Thus pH 7 is selected for subsequent samples detection for weakest fluorescence background and highest quenching efficiency.

The quantification of DOX was carried out using the optimized assay conditions. In Fig. 5 a, the sensor showed a strong dependence on the target concentration (0, 0.1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 20, 30 μM of DOX). Upon with the addition of DOX, the system exhibited significant fluorescence quenching of the CDs at 440 nm and enhancement of Eu3+ at 613 nm, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent response toward DOX. A linear fluorescence signal response (F613/F440) to DOX concentration was obtained over a wide range of 0.1–30 μM (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 43 nM, which was calculated by the equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 11) and k is the slope of the calibration curve. Besides, with DOX concentrations ranging from 0 to 30 μM, an apparent change of the color of the solution from blue to red was clearly observed with bare eyes under a UV lamp at 365 nm (the inset of Fig. 5a.). Accordingly, a novel visual ratiometric probe for DOX determination was successfully constructed. A comparison between analytical performances of proposed fluorescent probe with other fluorescence sensors reported in recent years for the detection of DOX was also made and the major findings were shown in Table S1. It is worthwhile noting that the overall performance of our sensor is superior. Although the linear range and LOD in this work is not the best, this method does have the advantages of simplicity in experimental procedure, visualization and excellent selectivity. In conclusion, this method can be well applied to the detection of DOX.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference of DOX sensor (Fig. 6 a), we studied the effects of 13 potential coexisting compounds on the fluorescence system and the results suggested that under the same detection conditions, although the concentration of these 13 substances (50 μM) was twice as much as that of DOX (25 μM), the fluorescence of the system did not change significantly. The selectivity apparently originated from the specific binding of Eu3+ to DOX. Besides, compared with the sample containing CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ + DOX, the mixtures of potential coexisting compounds in foods and CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ + DOX exhibited no remarkable fluorescence intensity changes, suggesting the potential of application in real food samples.

Moreover, we particularly compared the effects of other TCs (TC, OTC, TCT, and MOC, 30 μM) on the fluorescence system under the same conditions. Because of similar structure, other TCs except MOC can also quench the fluorescence of CDs and enhance the fluorescence of Eu3+. But interestingly, differ from other TCs, DOX exhibited the unique response signal to the fluorescence system. As shown in Fig. 6b, other TCs have obvious background interference under the same detection conditions,resulting in the final fluorescence color tend to orange or yellow which is obviously different from DOX under UV lamp. Besides, experiments were carried out without CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ under the same conditions, and we discovered that the fluorescence of TCs was quite weak, but the fluorescence of TCs was obviously enhanced after the addition of probe, may due to the combination of metal ions in probe and tetracycline compound. Similar finding was observed in other report [27]. In short, differences in structure of TCs resulted in diverse fluorescence response to this probe, leading to the certain selectivity for the detection of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in milk and honey samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the suitability of the proposed method in real food samples, milk and honey samples were analyzed with the standard addition calibration method. After pretreatment, 100 μL of the spiked food samples with appropriated amount of DOX were added to the fluorescence system and the fluorescence intensities at 440 and 613 nm were monitored under the same conditions as the linear detection. As shown in the Table 1 , the average recoveries were between 94.16 and 103.17% (RSD ≤ 2.23%), indicating that this fluorescence detection system has strong practical application value and can be applied to the detection of actual samples.

In summary, the highly water-soluble CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ nanocomposite were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal method and demonstrated to be a novel, rapid and selective fluorescent probe for DOX detection. Due to the IFE, DOX can quench the fluorescence of CDs, and based on the energy transfer, DOX can enhance the fluorescence of Eu3+. Therefore, a visual ratiometric detection of DOX can be established. The results showed that DOX concentration displayed a good linear relationship with the fluorescence ratio (F613/F440) in the range of 0.1–30 μM with a low LOD of 43 nM, which is comparable or superior to many other fluorescence sensors reported in recent years. Furthermore, compared to other TCs, the probe has certain selectivity for the detection of DOX, and has been successfully applied for the determination of DOX in milk and honey samples with average recoveries of 94.16–103.17% (RSD ≤ 2.23%). The CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ probe proposed in this paper is easy to synthesize, good water-soluble, and can detect DOX in foods rapidly, presenting potential application value in the field of food analysis. At the same time, the emergence of this new kind of nanoparticles composite also provides a new idea for the research and development of CDs - RE hybrid nanomaterials.
Xiaolin Tian: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Zhefeng Fan: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119925.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive sensing mercury in milk based on carbon quantum dots modified with europium (III) complexes
